skill.name: edn-analyzer
skill.description: Analyzes Logseq EDN template graphs for structure, quality, property usage, class hierarchy, and variant differences.
skill.summary: Review Logseq EDN templates and find structure, quality, and comparison insights.
skill.author: C0ntr0lledCha0s

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, network, and weak-crypto patterns. Review found these are mostly markdown examples, EDN keywords, Clojure snippets, and public reference links rather than malicious code. The skill still asks agents to read local files and optionally use Bash or grep, so it should publish with a clear external-command warning.

medium_findings.title: Optional Local Shell Command Use
medium_findings.description: The skill lists Bash as a tool and documents grep and shell-loop examples for local EDN analysis. This is a legitimate analysis workflow, but command execution can be risky if a user supplies untrusted paths or asks for unsafe shell changes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence directly shows Bash and grep usage in the skill documentation. Confidence is high that command execution is intended, but the context is local analysis rather than malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Local File Read and Write Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill tells agents to read EDN templates and the reference shows Clojure examples that slurp template files and spit output files. This is expected for EDN analysis, but users should review file targets before allowing writes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file operations are explicit and part of the documented workflow. The risk is moderate because the examples operate on local files and do not show destructive commands.

low_findings.title: Network Alerts Are Public Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to EDN, Schema.org, Logseq, and Clojure documentation. No evidence found that the skill sends data to these URLs or performs network requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are plain markdown reference links. There is no fetch, curl, webhook, credential transfer, or executable network code in the reviewed context.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Obfuscation Alerts Are Syntax False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-crypto and obfuscation alerts appear to match EDN keywords, markdown tables, Clojure forms, and formatting characters. No evidence found of hashing, encryption, encoded payloads, or hidden execution logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found documentation and examples at representative flagged locations. The scanner patterns are plausible false positives because the files are markdown reference material, not runnable obfuscated code.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Alerts Are Analysis Vocabulary
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alerts correspond to template validation terms, file-size discussion, and local module statistics. No evidence found of host fingerprinting, credential discovery, or unauthorized system inventory.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines describe EDN template sizes, module counts, and example growth reports. They do not enumerate host settings or sensitive local system data.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented Shell Snippets Require User Approval
dangerous_patterns.description: The reference includes shell snippets using grep, basename, command substitution, sort, uniq, and pipes. These should be treated as examples that require normal command review before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell snippets are real commands in documentation. They appear benign and local, but command substitution and pipelines are still execution patterns.

content.value_statement: Large Logseq EDN templates are hard to inspect by hand. This skill turns graph templates into clear structure, quality, and comparison reports.
actual_capabilities: Counts Logseq classes, properties, modules, and property types.
Finds orphaned classes, orphaned properties, duplicate titles, and missing fields.
Maps class hierarchies and property-to-class relationships.
Compares EDN template variants and highlights included or excluded structures.
Summarizes cardinality, property type, and module distribution patterns.
limitations: Designed for Logseq database graph EDN, not general Clojure application analysis.
Accuracy depends on readable EDN files and consistent template conventions.
May need Bash or grep for large local repositories if simple reading is slow.
Does not provide a full EDN parser inside the skill files.

use_cases.title: Audit Template Quality
use_cases.description: Check a graph template for orphaned classes, unused properties, duplicate names, and missing required fields before release.

use_cases.title: Compare Template Variants
use_cases.description: Compare full, CRM, research, or content templates to understand size differences and variant-specific entities.

use_cases.title: Understand Data Models
use_cases.description: Generate reports that explain class hierarchy, inherited properties, relationship fields, and module balance.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Template Summary
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this Logseq EDN template and summarize total classes, properties, property types, and any obvious quality issues.

prompt_templates.title: Find Quality Issues
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the EDN template for orphaned classes, orphaned properties, duplicate titles, missing fields, and broken references.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Two Variants
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare these two Logseq EDN template variants. Show class counts, property counts, unique items, and practical tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Deep Ontology Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform a deep analysis of hierarchy, property reuse, cardinality patterns, module balance, and recommendations for restructuring.

output_examples.input: Analyze a full Logseq EDN template.
output_examples.output: A concise report with total classes, total properties, property type distribution, cardinality distribution, top hierarchies, and quality warnings.

output_examples.input: Compare the full template with a CRM-focused variant.
output_examples.output: A side-by-side comparison showing size, class count, property count, included modules, excluded modules, and CRM-specific value.

output_examples.input: Find orphaned classes and unused properties.
output_examples.output: A prioritized issue list with affected entity names, likely cause, suggested parent or class assignment, and recommended next action.

best_practices: Start with a read-only summary before asking for fixes or generated files.
Provide both source and build EDN files when comparing generated templates.
Review any proposed Bash command before execution, especially with user-supplied paths.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill to analyze unrelated Clojure application code.
Do not ask it to modify many EDN files before reviewing the issue report.
Do not run shell snippets against untrusted paths without checking them first.

faq.question: What type of EDN does this skill support?
faq.answer: It is built for Logseq database graph templates with properties, classes, and ontology export markers.

faq.question: Can it compare different template versions?
faq.answer: Yes. It can compare variants and explain class, property, module, and feature differences.

faq.question: Does it fix template issues automatically?
faq.answer: It can suggest fixes. Apply changes only after reviewing the proposed parent, property, or module updates.

faq.question: Why does the audit mention command execution?
faq.answer: The skill documents Bash and grep examples for local analysis. Review commands before allowing execution.

faq.question: Does it contact external services?
faq.answer: No evidence found of network requests. The only reviewed URLs are public documentation links.

faq.question: Can it handle very large templates?
faq.answer: It includes guidance for large files, but performance depends on file size and available tool access.
